text: The surgeon carefully dissects the adhered small bowel from the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon, revealing a potential fistula adherence. The area is reinforced with interrupted trio silk sutures.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, entering the retroperitoneal plane. Ureteral catheters are used, and ICG is checked to ensure the ureter is away from the superior hemorrhoidal pedicle. The superior hemorrhoidal vessel is isolated and divided using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon performs a medial dissection of the vasculature, identifying and taking the superior hemorrhoidal vein. Due to the adherence of the sigmoid colon to the pelvic sidewall, the medial dissection is halted, and a lateral dissection is initiated. The surgeon progresses towards the splenic flexure, using scissors for the dissection before switching to a vessel sealer for dividing the lateral attachments.

The surgeon continues lateral mobilization, joining the medially dissected plane. A sponge is visible as the surgeon uses ICG to identify the ureter for confident dissection. The surgeon works to detach the sigmoid colon from the bladder, addressing chronic adherence without a fistula.

The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to check for green fluorescence inside the bladder, indicating potential injury during dissection. The suction device is used to clear the field, revealing granulation tissue near the bladder. The ICG confirms no bladder injury, and the surgeon navigates through the colon wall, observing granulation tissue suggestive of a developing fistula.

The surgeon completes the lateral dissection of the sigmoid colon, followed by a limited rectal mobilization to decrease tension in the anastomosis. The splenic flexure is released by lifting the mesentery off the kidney, using a lateral approach due to difficulty in repositioning the patient.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect near the spleen, pulling the colon to expose the high flexure tucked beneath the spleen. A capsular tear occurs, causing bleeding, which is controlled using bipolar cautery. The surgeon continues the lateral approach, lifting the descending colon, mesentery, and splenic flexure off the kidney while searching for the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon adjusts the table position to achieve a better view, entering the gastrocolic ligament and the lesser sac. The dissection continues around the pancreas, freeing the transverse colon mesentery from the inferior border of the pancreas. The splenic flexure is released, and the surgeon clears the distal mesentery using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon marks the proximal margin of the colon, noting the woody texture of the mesentery due to disease. The surgeon then divides the colon distally and proximally to ensure complete removal of the diseased section.
The surgeon performs a sewn anastomosis, creating a proctotomy and colotomy before suturing a two-layer anastomosis between the colon and rectum.

The surgeon places the final stitches in the anastomosis, then cleans the surgical area and floods the pelvis to perform a leak test. No bubbles are observed during the test, indicating a successful anastomosis.
